title: Pollutant Pathways Map
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive p5.js landscape cross-section where students trace how five pollutant types move through environmental pathways from source to receptor

learningObjectives: Trace how different pollutants move through environmental pathways from source to receptor
Distinguish between point source and nonpoint source pollution
Evaluate the effectiveness of treatment plants and wetland buffers for different pollutant types
prerequisites: Basic understanding of water flow in landscapes
Introduction to types of pollutants and their sources
Familiarity with point source and nonpoint source pollution concepts

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Spatial visualization with pollutant pathway tracing and toggle-based exploration
keyQuestions: Why are nonpoint source pollutants harder to regulate than point source pollutants?
How does a pollutant released at a factory upstream affect organisms in a lake downstream?
What role do wetland buffers play in intercepting pollutants before they reach waterways?
How does bioaccumulation cause pesticides to affect organisms far from the original source?
commonMisconceptions: All pollution comes from factories -- nonpoint source pollution from agriculture and urban runoff is often a larger contributor to water quality problems
Treatment plants remove all pollutants -- different treatment technologies target different contaminants, and some pollutants pass through conventional treatment
Pollutants stay where they are released -- water transport can carry contaminants far from their source, affecting ecosystems across an entire watershed
assessmentOpportunities: Have students create a comparison table of all five pollutant types covering source, pathway, and mitigation
Ask students to design a watershed management plan addressing both point and nonpoint sources
Use the toggle features to evaluate which mitigation infrastructure is most effective for each pollutant type
